content: Hi! I'm Dana, I work as a data engineer at a logistics startup in Turin. Can you help me pick between Airflow and Prefect for our pipelines?
Ciao Dana! For a small team, Prefect is lighter to operate; Airflow wins on ecosystem. How many pipelines and who maintains them?
About 20 pipelines, just me and one junior. We're a Python shop, everything runs on a single Hetzner box. Keep answers short please, I hate walls of text.
Prefect then: single box, two people, 20 pipelines. Airflow's operational overhead isn't worth it at your scale.
Deal, going with Prefect. Remind me to revisit this if we pass 100 pipelines.
My pandas ETL step takes 40 minutes on the daily shipment file (8GB CSV). Ideas?
Try polars with lazy scanning, or duckdb straight over the CSV. Both should cut it to minutes.
Tried polars, 40 min down to 4. I'm converting all our pandas jobs to polars, it's our standard now.
Nice 10x. Watch out for pandas-specific dtypes when porting; polars is stricter about nulls.
I use pandas for everything tabular, it's my default tool. Quick question: best way to read a 2GB parquet?
pd.read_parquet with pyarrow engine; select only the columns you need to keep memory down.
Thanks. Also, I prefer answers in English even though I'm Italian, my whole team reads English.
Noted, English it is.
We got GDPR questions from a client about our shipment tracking data. What's the minimum we need for a data retention policy?
Minimum: retention periods per data category, lawful basis, deletion procedure, and a named owner. Want a one-page template?
Yes, template please. This GDPR thing is still open, I need to get legal to review it — keep reminding me.
Template drafted. Open item: legal review of the retention policy.
I'm learning Rust on weekends, mostly for fun. Wrote my first CLI yesterday. Any project ideas that fit a data engineer?
A fast CSV profiler, a parquet inspector, or a tiny DSL for pipeline configs. All weekend-sized.
CSV profiler it is. I'll call it csvprof.
Our Postgres is at 80% disk. Biggest table is shipment_events, 400M rows. We never query older than 90 days.
Partition by month, detach and archive partitions older than 90 days to parquet on object storage. Want the DDL?
Yes. We decided: partitioning + parquet archive to Backblaze B2. That's the plan of record.
DDL and archive script provided. Decision recorded: monthly partitions, 90-day hot window, B2 archive.
The junior, Marco, keeps pushing straight to main. How do I set up branch protection without making him feel policed?
Frame it as protecting everyone including you. Require PRs + one review, keep CI fast. Pair with him on the first few PRs.
Good angle. Done, protection is on. He took it well.
Can you review my csvprof README? I want it punchy. I always sign my projects 'made with too much espresso'.
Send it over. And the espresso sign-off is a nice trademark, keep it.
Here it is. Also FYI I switched the whole team to uv for Python tooling, pip is banned.
README tightened. Noted: uv is the team standard for Python tooling.
Thinking about proposing a 4-day week experiment to my CEO. We're 12 people. How do I pitch it with data?
Pitch a 3-month trial with clear metrics: delivery cadence, incident rate, sick days. Offer a rollback condition upfront.
Drafting it now. Still unresolved, CEO wants to see Q2 numbers first. Park this until July.
Migrating shipment_events went great, disk is at 45%. Also csvprof got 50 GitHub stars this week!
Both wins. The partition plan paid off, and congrats on csvprof taking off.
Next up I want to try duckdb as the query layer over the B2 parquet archive. Add it to my list of things to explore.
On the list: duckdb over the parquet archive as a query layer.
